[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- main subject: animal head
- animal color: light brown/tan
- background: blurry, possibly indoor or abstract
- overall shape: rounded, organic
</features>
<kb>
- IF [main subject is an animal with fur and a distinct head shape] THEN [3]
- IF [main subject is a large vehicle with a cabin and a cargo area] THEN [9]
- IF [main subject is a car with headlights and a visible front grille] THEN [1]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 3 fires because the query image clearly shows the head of an animal with fur, which aligns with the features observed in the example for class 3 (a cat).
</rule_check>
<response>3</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- object shape: large, rectangular body with a distinct cab at the front
- object color: predominantly white with a dark grey/black bumper and lower front
- background: blurred outdoor setting, possibly a road or parking area
- visible details: front grille, windshield, and headlights are somewhat discernible
</features>
<kb>
- IF [object shape is a small, four-legged animal with fur AND/OR object color is black and white] THEN [3]
- IF [object shape is a large vehicle with a distinct cab and a rectangular cargo area AND/OR object color is predominantly white] THEN [9]
- IF [object shape is a sleek, low-profile vehicle with a rounded front AND/OR object color is light grey/silver] THEN [1]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 9 fires because the Query Image shows a large vehicle with a distinct cab and a rectangular cargo area, and its color is predominantly white, which matches the features described in the rule.
</rule_check>
<response>9</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- overall shape: elongated, rectangular body with rounded edges
- visible components: headlights, grille, wheels, side mirrors
- color: predominantly dark (black)
- background: indoor setting, possibly a showroom, with other blurred objects
</features>
<kb>
- IF [overall shape is elongated and rectangular with visible headlights, grille, and wheels] THEN [1]
- IF [overall shape is elongated and rectangular with a large cargo area or tank, and visible wheels] THEN [9]
- IF [overall shape is small, compact, and irregular with visible fur patterns] THEN [3]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 1 fires because the Query Image shows an object with an elongated and rectangular overall shape, and clearly visible headlights, a grille, and wheels, which are characteristic features of the examples in class 1.
</rule_check>
<response>1</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

What is the class of this image?
What is the class of this image?

Query Image:
Classify this image using only the labeled examples above.
Valid labels: [3, 9, 1]
Follow the exact XML structure required by the system instructions.
